data.title: Need Growing EPS And Dividends? Prescribe Sanofi
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

There's no doubt the world is getting older.

There has been a steady trend of the world getting older. Each year, fewer babies are born, and people are living longer. This leads to an older average population. The median age in Europe and North America, as well as the entire globe, continues to trend higher.

One reason for this is the success of modern medicine. We're able to treat and keep people alive longer and potentially healthier for longer as well. Some have doubted the ability of medicine to truly solve problems but primarily just mask them. Western medicine is often viewed as being focused on treating the symptoms and not the root cause of the problems, and this is probably a very valid critique in many cases. We take medicine to help the headache go away without ever figuring out what is causing the headache. Or we know what causes the headache but choose not to adjust our lifestyle and instead use medicine as a supplement to fix our potential problems.

Today, I want to focus on an investment opportunity within the pharmaceutical space. The healthcare sector can be broken down into categories that focus on insurance, service providers, and pharmaceuticals or other biosciences. Today's company is in the last group.

Let's dive in!

Deep Value And Healthy Growth In The Pharma Space

Sanofi (SNY) is one of the world's largest international drugmakers.

In 2025, the French pharmaceutical company's product portfolio included four blockbuster drugs and a blockbuster vaccine franchise. This was led by its smash-hit immunology drug called Dupixent, which put up 25.2% sales growth during the year to 15.71 billion euros. Its Beyfortus respiratory syncytial virus vaccine logged 1.80 billion euros in sales, which was up 9.5% over 2024.

The Lantus and Toujeo insulin medicines recorded 1.73 billion and 1.35 billion euros in sales in 2025, respectively. These were both double-digit percentage growth rates as well. Finally, Sanofi's rare disease (hemophilia drug) Altuviiio's sales soared 77.6% during the year to 1.16 billion euros.

So, the existing drug portfolio is clearly strong. What gives us confidence in the company's future moving forward is its pipeline (six in registration, 28 in phase 3 trials, and the rest were in early-stage trials). As of Jan. 29, 2026, Sanofi had 80 clinical-stage projects in its pipeline.

These include the immunology drug candidate Amlitelimab, multiple sclerosis drug candidate Fexalimab, and inflammatory bowel disease drug candidate Duvakitug. Each of these drugs is widely viewed by analysts as possessing blockbuster potential.

Beyond its pipeline, Sanofi has the financial means to be active on the M&A front. This is because it enjoys an AA S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. That gives it the low cost of capital to fill in most holes in its pipeline where needed. For these reasons, the analyst consensus is for Sanofi's EPS to rise by 9.3% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $4.60.

Based on current euro-to-USD exchange rates, the company offers a 5.1% dividend yield. Earlier this year, Sanofi raised its dividend for the 31st consecutive year (up 5.1% to 4.12 euros). With the payout ratio set to be in the high-40% range in 2026, the company could realistically keep hiking the dividend at a mid-single-digit rate annually. This could produce a Chowder number of 9 to possibly 10 in the years ahead.

Sanofi appears to be heavily discounted. At the current $46 share price, shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 9.2 (87% weight for 2026 and 13% weight for 2027). Put into perspective, this is well below the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/E ratio of roughly 13.

Moving forward, we think that a reasonable fair value for Sanofi remains in line with its 10-year average multiple. That's supported by the company's 9.3% annual EPS growth consensus, which is more than double the 10-year average CAGR of 4.2%.

Pair this with an AA S&P credit rating, and Sanofi's 10-year average P/E ratio can be defended in our view. This suggests the stock is trading at a 29% discount to our $65 fair value per share estimate.

If Sanofi meets growth projections and reverts to fair value, it could deliver a 44% upside in the remainder of the calendar year. By the end of 2031, the stock could post 16% annual total returns.

Pertinent risks facing Sanofi include the loss of exclusivity of Dupixent early next decade and drug pricing legislation.

The company must prove to the market that it can fill the hole that will be left when the Dupixent patent expires in key markets in the early 2030s. If its internal pipeline and bolt-on acquisitions fail to deliver, Sanofi's growth potential could be undermined.

What's more, the company faces the risk of blockbuster drugs eventually being selected by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services for drug price negotiations under the Inflation Reduction Act. That could lower prices on impacted drugs in the United States, which could impact Sanofi's financial results.

Conclusion

Sanofi allows you to invest in a company that supports the modern-age population. Ever hear of the "Silver Tsunami?" There was a belief that we needed to massively invest in nursing homes or other senior nursing facilities within the United States. The idea was that there was going to be this massive wave of retirees who were crashing into the system and needed more care; thus, providers would see growing profits from a burgeoning demand.

The reality is that many seniors are choosing to stay home longer than their parents did. They're refusing to lose their individuality and their self-care because they're healthier for longer. How is this possible? One, partially by the lifestyles that they choose to live, and two, because of the prevalence and benefits of modern medicine. Recognizing that medicine plays a key role in the well-being of countless retirees and non-retirees who need medicine's intervention to protect them or help them from modern ailments, Sanofi, as one of the largest producers of pharmaceuticals in the world, allows you to benefit from this growing trend.

You might be young, sprightly, and not need much medication today. I hope that for as long as possible, you don't need medication to supplement or aid your well-being. But I also hope that when you do need it, it will be available and ready to help you, perhaps even better than what is available today. When you're building a dividend portfolio and a portfolio that's going to provide you with financial security through retirement, investing in the reality of the situation can help you benefit now but also tomorrow. That's the beauty of being able to take a step back and gather clarity amidst the chaos of day-to-day life. This is exactly what we do within the Dividend Kings.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Olaplex: The Turnaround Is Lagging
data.description:
Olaplex Holdings, Inc. (OLPX) reported fairly weak Q4 results, sending the stock plummeting by nearly -18%. The haircare company’s brand turnaround story hasn’t shown much progress in spite of massive marketing investments and a brand image update, questioning Olaplex’s future growth. Sluggish 2026 guidance weighs on the market sentiment while Olaplex’s brand has continued to struggle.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous January article on the stock, titled “Olaplex: Takeover Rumors Arise.” The stock has since lost -12% of its value; meanwhile, the S&P 500 has lost -1%.

Olaplex Q4 Review: The Turnaround Is Lagging

Olaplex’s growth turnaround strategy isn’t showing positive signals yet. The Q4 report showed financials in line with Wall Street expectations for the quarter, but not much can still be expected going forward. Olaplex's brand has continued to struggle.

Revenues came in at $105.1 million for Q4, up by 4.3% year-on-year. Revenues plummeted by -14.5% in the specialty retail channel as retailer sell-through seemed to remain subdued, according to earnings call remarks. Healthier 6.6% growth in direct-to-consumer and good 18.9% growth in the professional channel led total growth to come in at a modest but positive level. The two latter sales channels’ growth is more important for Olaplex at the current revenue mix.

I believe that Q4 revenue growth was still relatively disappointing. Olaplex has invested very heavily in marketing efforts throughout 2025 to re-elevate the brand, but results have so far remained very modest. A renewed brand image and significant marketing campaigns haven’t yet improved the consumer perception due to lingering brand backlash. Adjusted SG&A expenses grew by 22.1% to $61.4 million in Q4 primarily due to increased marketing, following a similar pattern from previous quarters in 2025. Even though Olaplex managed to increase the gross margin by 200 basis points to 70.6%, adjusted EBITDA declined by -26.5% to $12.9 million. Bottom line results don’t suggest any success in Olaplex’s turnaround yet.

Such slow growth was ultimately already guided for Q4, but Olaplex’s new 2026 guidance also suggests very sluggish growth for the current year. I believe that the 2026 guidance was the weakest signal in the Q4 report. Revenues are guided at $414-435 million, implying only -2.1% to 2.8% growth from 2025. At the midpoint, revenue growth isn’t even following inflation, even though Olaplex has spent a massive amount on marketing. Adjusted EBITDA is guided at a 21-22% margin for the year, declining slightly from 22.2% in 2025. Marketing investments seem to be lasting at a very high level but still are not revitalizing growth.

Olaplex notes a cautious macroeconomic outlook to impact the 2026 outlook, but I believe that hair care is a very defensive product category, and that macroeconomic pressure isn’t the only reason for such weak growth guidance. Weak consumer confidence likely has a small, but relatively modest negative impact on industry-wide demand for the year. L’Oréal S.A. (LRLCF) reported 6.0% revenue growth for Q4 and notes an optimistic outlook for the global beauty market for 2026. Unilever PLC (UL) expects 4-6% organic growth for 2026 while noting some macroeconomic pressure.

Olaplex assumes first quarter growth to even fall below the full-year guidance algorithm, and that EBITDA will be pressured due to front-loaded marketing expenses during the quarter. The 2026 guidance implies that Olaplex’s marketing efficiency will improve throughout the year, leaving stronger expectations for later quarters. Such comments even question Olaplex's ability to grow in line with the company's guidance during 2026. Overall, the brand turnaround stands on very volatile ground after the report.

OLPX Stock Isn’t Attractive Without Growth

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model expectations. I flattened my revenue growth expectations for Olaplex to 2% growth from 2027 forward, reflecting the company’s issues in revitalizing the brand.

I again assume the adjusted EBITDA margin to stabilize at 20%, as even maintaining sales looks to require massive marketing expenses.

I have kept my cash flow conversion estimates the same, not assuming significant CapEx or other cash items past taxes.

The estimates put Olaplex’s fair value at $1.34, suggesting 9% downside as of writing. There isn’t an attractive investment case without growth reacceleration, and no signals of such reacceleration have been shown for now. I believe that investors are much better off waiting for improved turnaround signals, or an even lower stock price.

The fair value estimate is down from $1.43 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.05%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate an 8.57% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I again assume a fairly moderate 20% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.15% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.21. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 9.71% and the WACC at 9.05%.

Takeaway

The Q4 report sent negative signals regarding Olaplex’s brand turnaround; despite massive marketing investments, sales continue to lag and are guided to continue to do so in 2026. Profitability has declined significantly due to marketing investments.

Without a buyout offer that hasn’t materialized yet, or improved growth as a standalone company, I believe that OLPX stock isn’t an attractive investment. I remain at a Hold rating and wait for better signals.

data.title: Netflix: Extended Gains Seem Likely
data.description:
Netflix Inc.'s (NFLX) stock has received plenty of attention lately, given its involvement in acquisition dealings and broader volatility among tech stocks. I've been bullish about Netflix's prospects since September 2023 and reiterated my bullish outlook in late 2024 on the basis of momentum in key growth variables. Upon reassessing Netflix's prospects, I decided to maintain my bullish outlook; here's why.

Starting With The Obvious

Warner Bros. & Acquisition Premium Release

Netflix announced an agreement to acquire Warner Bros. (WBD) in December, which weighed on its stock price, largely due to the market's concern of an embedded acquisition premium. However, Netflix's stock eventually recovered in February-March, rallying by double digits after the acquisition had been cancelled due to a higher bid by Paramount (PSKY), which Netflix failed to match. In addition, Netflix is set to receive a $2.8 billion termination fee as part of the deal falling through, which adds additional liquidity to its balance sheet.

An acquirer's stock usually sells off most abruptly pre- or post-announcement of an acquisition (assuming an embedded premium), instead of on completion dates. A follow-up bidding war can lead to additional drawdowns. However, I always felt that Netflix would feel the brunt of the burden upon the announcement, especially as tech stocks were systematically underperforming at the time (a secondary layer). A deal cancellation led to Netflix's stock price soaring on the basis of a premium recoup and cash collection. In addition, it seemed like the market held concerns about integration costs, which spurred on additional price movement when the deal was cancelled.

Long story short, Netflix will no longer be acquiring Warner Bros., it will no longer commit to a premium, and the market has priced the news favorably. Although it has subsequently decided to acquire Ben Affleck's filmmaking tech company InterPositive for an undisclosed sum, the acquisition suggests it being part of a broader ~$20 billion 2026 spending plan and not a material corporate event.

The question now becomes: Will Netflix stock's recent surge evolve into a larger trend, or are we looking at short-term mean reversion? I believe in the prior; I explain why in the following section.

Why I still see value in Netflix

Short-Term Mean-Reversion Has Yet To Be Realized

Netflix's stock has increased by over 25% in its last six trading sessions (at the time of writing this article). However, the stock remains ~26.44% below its peak. Although the first pass has been priced-in, I highly doubt we've seen the end of Netflix's reversion.

As previously mentioned, Netflix's recent below-par stock performance can partially be attributed to the struggling tech sector. As illustrated by the following diagram, the Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund ETF (VGT) has underperformed both the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) in the past three months as part of a cyclical rotation. I wouldn't mind buying tech stocks into the rotation to lower the cost basis.

Longer-Term Fundamental Drivers

Quantitative Metrics

Netflix maintains robust and improving quantamental metrics. For example, its return on equity of 25.81% exceeds its equity cost of capital (aka CAPM). In addition, the company's return on invested capital reflects an excess over the market-based required return on total capital (Netflix's WACC is currently around 7.17%).

Margins have grown strongly, with gross profitability enhancements reflecting economies of scale. EBITDA reflects clean and growing profitability margins—I emphasize EBITDA, given that Netflix is a tech company with a substantial intangible asset load and volatile capital structure. Balance sheet metrics are elaborated upon later in the article.

Market pricing variables are shown in figure 5, illustrating that Netflix's salient forward multiples mostly trade below their 5-year normalized values. I find this highly encouraging, given the company's growing profit margins and evolving fundamentals (discussed in the next section). Some might question the elevated nature of these multiples. However, it should be considered that growth-stage firms that have consolidated market share can display elevated multiples. For the time being, I prefer comparing Netflix's point-in-time multiples to its non-cyclical multiples.

Qualitative Discussion

Figure 6 provides a short-run time series of Netflix's headline growth. The company's top and bottom-line items have enjoyed double-digit gains in recent quarters, mainly attributed to its substantial video streaming market share, which currently stands at ~21% in the U.S. and ~24% in Canada. In addition, the company has managed to curb the influence of password sharing by enhancing emphasis on IP addresses, adding fairly-priced shared account offerings, increasing advertising revenue (+$1.5 billion in 2025), and maintaining secular subscriber growth by delivering a variety of content geared to its end-user's behavior (Netflix currently has over 325 million paid users). Moreover, Netflix has phased in peripheral offerings such as gaming and live events, which bolster cross-selling and enhance engagement - we aren't looking at a platform that relies on a static business model.

Furthermore, the company's profitability growth remains clean. In fact, EPS is anticipated to settle at 76 cents per share in Q1 2026, up from 56 cents per share in Q4 2025. Profitability can be attributed to clean P&L management and power over content suppliers—Netflix's content amortization growth will settle at ~10% for Q1, which has been a large contributor to profit and loss management.

One of Netflix's many benefits is its global reach. Aside from holding strong streaming market share in parts of developed Asia (~21.7% in Japan), Netflix has accessed emerging markets, many of which are dominated by a select few players, including Netflix. Regions such as LATAM and Africa have tremendous potential, given the population density and consolidation of middle classes. A few concerns would be that FX translation can be challenging. Moreover, Netflix still hasn't featured directly in China, which is a substantial barrier to any tech company seeking secular growth. That said, Netflix has featured strongly in India, an overlooked EM with substantial growth prospects.

Figure 7 quantifies much of what I highlighted in the previous paragraph. In addition, it shows resilient growth in developed market regions, especially UCAN, which is the company's ballast. Momentum from content including Stranger Things, David Chapelle, anime (geared toward Japan) has been met with frequent new releases to sustain momentum. The fallback would be customer loyalty and pricing.

Figure 8 shows a compact version of Netflix's balance sheet. As previously mentioned, the company is set to receive $2.8 billion in cash back from Discovery, after the firm decided to sell Warner Bros. to Paramount post-agreement. The cash will provide a healthy add-on to the existing ~$9 billion in cash & equivalents. However, more importantly, Netflix hasn't shown signs of solvency risk. While its substantial intangible asset load needs to be considered, the company has a total assets-to-total debt ratio of ~1.92x, diminishing short-term debt, and growing retained earnings.

Noteworthy Risk Factors

Stocks often perform systematically. In other words, if tech stocks fall abruptly, you'll likely see Netflix follow suit, unless another corporate event occurs.

Another risk worth considering is Netflix's participation in a competitive industry. Rising competition might dilute the company's capacity and profits, which could be problematic, seeing as it doesn't have a tangible asset base to fall back on.

Lastly, the stock has experienced significant max drawdown in the past, losing as much as 81.99% of its value. Left-tail risk remains high, despite the company's recent market share consolidation.

Conclusion

I am bullish about Netflix's long-run prospects. Although its recent spike in performance can be attributed to the Warner Bros.' fall-through, fundamental indicators, price multiples, and finer nuances suggest that the stock might deliver stellar performance for years to come.

The company's double-digit growth will be difficult to derail, especially given its emerging market expansion, unit economics, and continuously developing content feed. Risks such as a growing competitive environment, a large intangible asset base, and telling left tail risk ought to be considered, but don't show signs of outweighing the positives.

data.title: Ciena Q1 Earnings Review: Reiterate Buy Rating
data.description:
Stock markets did not react well to Ciena (CIEN), despite the optical networking company reporting strong fiscal fourth quarter results. Ciena also raised its forecast, but the stock closed at $299.30, down by 12.88%. The company is among the firms that benefit from Nvidia’s (NVDA) halo effect. Coherent (COHR), Lumentum (LITE), and Corning (GLW) are also on that list.

Assuming for a moment that the post-earnings drop in CIEN stock is due to the Dow Jones (DJI) falling by up to 1,000 points on Thursday and Nasdaq (QQQ) at around an intraday low of 22,500, what should shareholders like about Ciena’s quarterly results?

First Quarter Exceeded Forecast

Ciena posted an adjusted earnings per share of $1.35. It beat estimates by $0.18. Revenue of $1.43 billion (+33.6% Y/Y) exceeded estimates by $33 million. Chief Financial Officer Marc Graff said that the company ended the quarter with a strong balance sheet. It also exercised financial discipline yet met growing demand, increasing profitability and shareholder returns.

Chief Executive Officer Gary Smith said that Ciena has industry-leading technology and is in a position to meet multi-year demand. The AI-driven networking continued to scale. He elaborated on the conference call, mentioning the market share growth as AI-driven connectivity customer spending increased. Both service and cloud provider customers required connectivity through managed optical fiber networks (“MOFN”).

In India, orders increased by 40% Y/Y, led by MOFM demand. WAN connectivity requirements also contributed to growth in that country.

In the data center, Ciena pointed to opportunities in the out-of-band management (its “DCOM” solution) product. It is leveraging the XGS-PON (10-Gigabit Symmetrical Passive Optical Network), along with the routing and switching platforms, for the hyperscale provisioning and configuration segments. That added to its backlog.

CFO Graff said that a strong order book and record Q1 backlogs support durable demand this year and into 2027.

Adjusted EBITDA

In the adjusted EBITDA section, Ciena’s stock-based compensation expense of $49.83 million is notable. It accounted for the biggest adjustment of EBITDA.

Outlook

Ciena issued a revenue forecast in the range of $1.5 billion. Adjusted (non-GAAP) operating margin is between 17.5% and 18.5%. The company adjusted its operating expense outlook to $1.52 billion to $1.53 billion. This narrow range reflects the benefit from a stronger demand environment.

The strong backlog consisted of 80% in products and software. 20% comes from services. MOFN, service providers, submarines, and hyperscalers are contributors to the backlog growth.

The RPO (remaining performance obligations) as a percentage of orders is 60%.

Opportunities For Ciena

Margin growth is a reason that CIEN stock should trend higher. Expect Ciena to expand its operating margins in the quarter ahead. It held OpEx growth flat year-on-year. CFO Graff said that it increased performance in the last year. It applied that increase by reinvesting back into the business. Ending investments in its 25-gig PON activity also contributed to the flat OpEx.

Ciena will continue to avoid a reliance on electronic manufacturing services (“EMS”). Instead, it is optimizing its supply chain and achieving vertical integration to reduce costs.

Competition is not a risk in owning Ciena shares. The company has close relationships with hyperscalers. That contributed to the order growth. In the data center market, the company offers an industry-leading high-speed connectivity solution. Staff have systems knowledge that improves customer satisfaction rates.

Despite having large competitors in the industry, Ciena has DCOM products that data centers require.

Risks

Data center and hyperscaler customers are growth drivers that lifted AI-related names. Nebius (NBIS), CoreWeave (CRWV), Corning, Coherent, and Lumentum are some examples. If demand for AI-related offerings suddenly weakens, customers might cut their orders.

Fortunately, companies like Ultra Clean (UCTT) said that orders are so strong that it needs to increase its capacity utilization.

Stock Grades

Ciena’s buy rating fell to a hold on February 19.

The valuation grade fell to a “D,” but investors get to own a stock with strong growth, momentum, profitability, and earnings/revenue revisions.

The EPS revision is large. Analysts are expecting a 131.59% Y/Y increase in EPS this year.

Your Takeaway

Posted last December as a “Buy,” Ciena retains that rating after its first-quarter report. Shares likely fell after pricing in the expectedly strong results. Shares rose by 48% in the last quarter and are up by nearly 300% in the last year. Macro volatility might worsen on matters not related to Ciena’s prospects. That might slow the stock’s uptrend that started last September.

data.title: Options To Address The Spike As Oil Breaches $80
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Jobs Day: Don't expect February's nonfarm payrolls report to repeat January's upside surprise.

AI feud: The Pentagon has designated Anthropic as a "supply chain risk," prompting the startup behind Claude to challenge the label in court.

The response: History says the Fed is key to counter a recession from oil shocks, but any reaction to the Iran war will likely depend on duration.

Black gold

Crude oil has officially topped $80 per barrel as the conflict in Iran continues to escalate. The WTI benchmark (CL1:COM) has surged 21% since closing at $67.02 just before the war commenced last Saturday amid increased risks to the global oil trade and the Strait of Hormuz. A fifth of the world's oil and gas flows through the strategic waterway, but tanker traffic there has ground to a halt, with hundreds of ships trapped on each side of the critical chokepoint. China presses Iran for safe passage

Snapshot: The national average cost of gasoline has additionally climbed by 34 cents over the past week to $3.32 per gallon, according to AAA, erasing all of the savings seen since President Trump took office. "If they rise, they rise," Trump declared in response to the spike in energy prices, saying "they'll drop very rapidly when this is over" and the current campaign is "far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit." For now, the bet is that a 4-to-5-week conflict will prove the spike is temporary, but emergency measures are being entertained if things get prolonged or prices spiral out of control. U.S. must have a role in choosing Iran's next leader

The first plan announced by the administration is to provide insurance guarantees and U.S. Navy escorts for oil tankers and other vessels traveling through the Strait of Hormuz. Other options under consideration range from a federal gasoline tax holiday and higher ethanol blends to having the U.S. Treasury trade oil futures and drawing from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (which is hovering at 60% capacity after being heavily tapped by the Biden administration during the Ukraine war). Looking to ease global supply, the U.S. also just issued a 30-day waiver to India for purchasing crude from Russia. Shipping industry sees pledge to reopen Hormuz as only partial fix

Elsewhere: Oil is not the only commodity that has policymakers worried about an energy crunch. While the U.S. is largely insulated against natural gas disruptions due to its large domestic production, alarm bells are going off in Europe as prices jumped 70% over the past week. It comes as the highly import-dependent EU was about to phase in a ban on Russian LNG imports, while climate ambitions that saw the bloc pivot away from nuclear and coal might be a wake-up call for carbon credits and its broader emissions trading system. (6 comments)

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Top 10 Defense Stocks As Mideast Conflict Escalates

Rethinking Long-Term Investing

If We're At War, Buy Nvidia

SCHD: Aren't You Glad You Didn't Sell?

Software Likely Bottomed Last Week: 3 Companies I'm Buying

What else is happening...

Report: UAE weighs freezing billions of dollars of Iranian assets.

Broadcom's (AVGO) outlook shows the AI party isn't slowing down.

Marvell (MRVL) shoots up as earnings and guidance beat estimates.

Robinhood (HOOD) enters premium credit card battle with new launch.

Trump removes Kristi Noem as DHS head after congressional clashes.

Beyond oil deals: U.S. and Venezuela re-establish diplomatic ties.

Oracle (ORCL) to fire thousands due to AI data center buildout costs.

China approves Pfizer's (PFE) GLP-1 drug for weight management.

Airline stocks lose altitude amid Middle East disruptions, fuel surge.

U.S. weighs permits for Nvidia (NVDA), AMD global AI chip sales.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +0.6%. Hong Kong +1.7%. China +0.4%. India -1.4%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.3%. Paris -0.5%. Frankfurt -0.2%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.2%. S&P -0.3%. Nasdaq -0.4%. Crude +4% to $84.26. Gold +0.5% to $5,102.30. Bitcoin -3.5% to $70,565.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +3 bps to 4.17%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Embraer (EMBJ) and Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Marriott Vacations: Still Keeping My Distance
data.description:
At some point in early 2025, I was intrigued by how richly valued the hotel companies were, namely Marriott (MAR), Hilton (HLT), and InterContinental (IHG). While all were trading at something close to a 30x price-to-earnings ratio (P/E), I found their 'siblings,' like Marriott Vacations (VAC) and Hilton Vacations (HGV), trading at much more reasonable valuations. I soon found out what I believe to be the reason and decided to write my first article about Marriott Vacations.

Since then I believe my analysis has been largely correct, as the stock has wobbled up and down and has failed to generate meaningful positive return in almost a year. While the S&P 500 is up by more than 15%, VAC is flat. My expectation is that this underperformance will continue, with some moments of more pronounced under- or overperformance, as we have seen since May 2025. I'll revisit and update my graphs from the first article, so first-time readers won't miss anything if they haven't read the previous analysis.

In sum, I'll keep my Hold rating intact because although I don't see much downside for Marriott Vacations, I have even less clarity about its upside. Revenue needs to start growing again for this company to generate more net income and free cash flow; otherwise, it won't be able to sustain its shareholder return strategy. Marriott Vacations is a Hold for now.

The Industry Is in Trouble

Throughout much of the 2010s, the timeshare market grew at a 6% CAGR (page 16 of the 2019 Investor Day Presentation), a period that also saw significant consolidation through mergers and acquisitions. Marriott Vacations was able to more than double its revenue between 2016 and 2019, in large part due to its acquisition of ILG. Now, growth engines are sputtering. The company hasn't shared in its Q4'25 earnings presentation any guidance that is close to the 6% CAGR talked about pre-pandemic. It actually guided for contract sales to grow by just 1% in 2026 (page 24 of the Q4'25 earnings presentation).

Assuming pricing will be kept flat year-over-year, then 1% growth is basically driven by an increase in owners. With 700,000 owner families at the end of 2025, 1% means a net growth in ownership of just 7,000. The problem is that since the end of 2021, Marriott Vacations has been unable to grow owner families, staying flat at 700,000 owner families. Their claim of 100,000 new first-time buyers doesn't mean ~20,000 per year because between 2020 and 2021 the company increased from 650,000 to 700,000 owner families. If the assumption is that the company added 50,000 in 2021 and then 50,000 until 2025, it means around ~16,000 per year with a corresponding 16,000 finalizing their ownership contract. To add 7,000 on top of their current ownership, Marriott Vacations needs to increase its customer acquisition by almost 50% to 23,000.

The 23,000 new owners would roughly match what the company achieved pre-pandemic, as they added 95,000 new first-time buyers between 2016 and 2019 (~24,000 per year). But note how the share of generations has changed: while boomers accounted for 57% until 2019, now they account for just 30%, and millennials and Gen X account for 65%.

While at first glance this looks great because they are attracting a younger generation, it isn't because of the lower amount of first-time buyers I just mentioned (24,000 pre-pandemic compared to 16,000 in recent years). There are basically two explanations for this phenomenon: millennials and Gen X aren't as interested in owning timeshares as boomers were, and boomers are not renewing their ownership since the oldest boomer is now almost 80 years old. I think the answer lies somewhere between the two, and that's why I think Marriott Vacations will have a hard time growing its owner families number, meaning subdued revenue growth in the coming years.

No Growth, High Debt, and Fairly Valued

In my first article I argued that while the company had been able to generate sustainable free cash flow to support dividends and buybacks until 2022, that was no longer true afterward. I'll update the graphs previously shared with full data for 2025 and confirm that this continues to be the case. First, as anticipated by the lack of growth in owner families, revenue has been flat since 2022, while gross margin % has decreased by more than 7.0p.

Net income has also decreased significantly from its peak in 2022. If I were to remove the $546M non-cash impairment charges from Q4'25 and consider a normal tax rate, net income for 2025 would probably be around $200M - flat to down compared to previous years. 2022 was probably an exception year given the new contract sales and lower usage due to remaining pandemic restrictions. The ~$200M seems like its new net income trend.

Also impacting its profitability is the continued increase in net debt and net debt/EBITDA. Debt issuance continues to outpace any debt repayment or cash increase and now sits at almost $5.3B. This translates to a leverage of 9.1x net debt to EBITDA, which is extremely high when compared to pre-ILG acquisition and the first years after the pandemic.

All that increase in net debt means the company now has almost 75% of its capital funded by debt, with the remaining 25% using equity. This is a significant change from pre-pandemic years that had less than 60% of debt-to-capital exposure. There is a limit to how much debt a company can issue (and therefore increase the proportion of debt to capital), and I believe we are close to this limit. Interest expense is also now consuming 5.1% of revenue compared to less than 1% previous to the ILG acquisition, even at a moment when interest rates are decreasing.

Finally, the last piece of the argument is Marriott Vacations' cash flow. Between 2016 and 2022, the company generated significant cash from operations (almost $2B), enough to cover capital expenditures, dividends, and a significant portion of its share buyback program. It used debt issuance to boost share buyback.

Since 2023, the situation has inverted. The company generated $465M in cash from operations, which is not enough to pay for both dividends and capital expenditures. Share buybacks have been entirely funded by debt issuance and cash on hand.

In 2026 I'm not expecting the situation to be that different, although the company has significantly decreased capital expenditures in recent years to avoid cutting its dividend. My adjusted free cash flow forecast is $121M based on a return to normality of cash from operations, $73M in capital expenditures (guided by the company), and $38M in share-based compensation (same as 2025). Since the company distributes around $110M in dividends each year, there is basically no cash left for other uses.

Yet, I'm not expecting the company to cut its dividend soon. In part because they disclosed an expectation of $200M to $250M on non-core asset sales in the next two years, which should boost the $121M I calculated by another ~$115M in each year.

In any case, my conclusion is that this company now needs to sell assets in order to fund its shareholder return strategy. Based on current free cash flow generation, it's very difficult to start paying down debt and do meaningful share buybacks. The dividend is safe for now, but I'm not sure for how long.

Given all discussed above, I can't give this company a rich valuation. Considering a normalized $200M net income, Marriott Vacations trades at almost 14x price-to-earnings ratio. For a company that has barely seen any revenue growth recently and is unlikely to see it in the coming years, I think 14x is actually very generous. It's even worse when valuing through free cash flow. Generating $121M of cash means it trades at almost 20x price to free cash flow. Again, too much for a company that doesn't grow, can't repay its debt, and is unlikely to boost share buybacks any time soon.

There Is Basically One Reason I Might Be Wrong

My take here is not that the company will fall in value in the coming months, but rather that upside is very limited. What could prompt this assessment to change is if the company improves its revenue growth. If Marriott Vacations starts growing revenue at the same clip as pre-pandemic, say ~5%, then that means cash flow from operations can be higher than it is now, allowing debt repayment or share buyback.

So any sign that the new CEO and COO are turning the business around may be bullish for the company. However, I need to stress that I believe the industry itself is in trouble, so it's difficult to see how much two people can really improve the situation.

It's a Hold For Now

In the end, I'm somewhere between a Sell and a Hold rating, but I'll keep my Hold rating as is because I don't see a significant downside to this company. Still, I continue to see limited upside, and I prefer to keep my distance. I'll wait for more commercial improvements and better revenue results before thinking about purchasing Marriott Vacations.

data.title: 2 Best-Of-Breed Monthly Retirement Dividend Machines Yielding 10-11%
data.description:
Being able to generate significant passive income every month makes retirement so much easier because it means you can largely ignore market volatility and instead sit back, relax, and let the dividends flow. Additionally, if you're able to generate that yield from a dependable source that comes at a 10% rate or greater, you can rapidly accelerate your path to retiring on dividends.

Of course, I do not personally suggest building your entire portfolio of 10% yielding securities, but putting some of them in a well-diversified portfolio that also includes some perhaps lower-yielding but fast-growing dividends can create a well-diversified portfolio that balances current yield with dividend growth over time in dividend stocks (VIG).

Certainly, the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) can provide a strong foundation for a dividend portfolio, with a 3.5% dividend yield, double-digit annualized dividend growth over the past decade, and broad diversification across over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks spread across numerous sectors. In this article, however, I'm going to share how to augment SCHD with two monthly dividend machines that yield 10% or more.

A 10%+ Monthly Dividend Machine With Mega-Cap Tech Exposure

The first opportunity I'm going to discuss is the Goldman Sachs NASDAQ 100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ). GPIQ is a compelling monthly dividend machine because it yields 10.22% on a trailing 12-month basis while also delivering capital appreciation over that period of time. Additionally, it has one of the lowest expense ratios among covered call premium covered call ETFs, with a 0.29% expense ratio as per Seeking Alpha. That said, this includes a waiver that will soon go away, which will bring its expense ratio up to 0.35%. It will still be in line with the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), which it has outperformed over time, and also be significantly lower than peers like the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), with a 0.68% expense ratio, as well as the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), which charges a 0.6% expense ratio.

The biggest reason to have GPIQ in a portfolio, in my view, besides the attractive monthly payout, is the fact that it provides concentrated exposure to mega-cap tech stocks in technology stocks (XLK). It has over 50% of its portfolio in technology stocks, including NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Broadcom (AVGO) in its top ten holdings.

The reason this is such a big deal is that most income-focused strategies typically lack exposure to mega-cap tech stocks, since they tend to pay out very small dividends. However, GPIQ tries to bring the best of both worlds to income investors by implementing a covered call strategy on top of its underlying NASDAQ 100 holdings through the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ).

Of course, the drawback to this is that implementing covered calls caps your upside substantially, which is potentially disastrous when investing in tech stocks that tend to be quite volatile and very appreciation-oriented over time. This is evidenced by how dramatically QYLD has underperformed QQQ over the long term, including seeing its share price decline substantially over the long term despite its underlying index enjoying significant appreciation during that period.

However, GPIQ uses active management to only cover between 30% and 70% of its underlying holdings with calls at any given time. It dynamically manages those calls and will occasionally even buy calls to preserve some upside potential during market rallies. So far, it has done a pretty good job of this, as evidenced by its total return performance over time.

However, it is still important to realize that this strategy is not foolproof in that active management makes mistakes. Unfortunately, the higher fees that you pay for this as opposed to simply owning QQQ will further erode returns over time.

Thus, I do not think that GPIQ is a great pick for those focused on maximizing total returns and would therefore want to keep it a small portion of my portfolio only if I were an income investor who needed income from that capital and wanted to further diversify my portfolio. Still, given that I believe it is designed much better than many of its peers due to its lower expense ratio and active management that protects its upside potential, thereby combining the best traits of GPIQ and QQQI, I think it is worth considering for a retiree who's looking to diversify their portfolio to include mega-cap tech and enjoy an attractive monthly yield.

A Proven Double-Digit Yield From A High-Quality BDC

Another opportunity I think is attractive for monthly double-digit income yields is Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC). CSWC is a business development company (BIZD) that lends to middle-market companies.

Some important things to keep in mind about it are that it is internally managed, which enables it to generate a higher return on equity than many of its externally managed peers, like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), because of its lower and more efficient cost structure.

CSWC also has a tremendous track record of generating value for shareholders over the long term, as its total return track record clearly demonstrates.

It has also been performing well lately. In the most recent quarter, it increased its NAV per share while also paying out significant dividends through a $0.58-per-share regular dividend and a $0.06-per-share supplemental dividend.

Its portfolio is also in strong shape, with only 1.6% of it rated 4 or 5 on a risk basis, with the vast majority of it rated 1 or 2, which are its two strongest credit rating performances.

Additionally, it is defensively positioned, with 90% of the portfolio invested in first lien senior secured loans, and it does not have much of anything in the way of software exposure, which sets it apart from a lot of its peers. Given that software is out of favor right now and considered a riskier asset class, this further boosts CSWC's risk-reward profile.

Finally, it has a very strong balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating, no debt maturities until 2028, and no fixed-rate debt maturities until 2029 or later. This insulates it pretty well from a lot of interest rate risk on its liabilities side of the equation.

It also has very few non-accruals, with only 1.5% of the portfolio on non-accrual at fair value. Its PIK income also remains very low, as 93% of its investment income is cash income, with it generating $0.60 in investment income per weighted average basic share this past quarter.

Importantly, it fully covers its base dividend of $0.58 per share. Given that it can continue issuing equity accretively, it should be able to sustain its net investment income per share quite well, as it did over this past year, thereby supporting its base dividend quite nicely.

Thus, it is possible that if the Federal Reserve cuts rates aggressively and the economy weakens, CSWC would have to cut its base dividend. I do not think that is likely, thereby making its double-digit yield very attractive right now for investors looking for a sustainable double-digit yield from a proven long-term compounder.

Why These High-Yield Monthly Income Machines Can Complement SCHD

It is important to keep in mind that neither of these investments is risk-free, as GPIQ faces market timing risks due to its covered call and actively managed strategy, as well as significant concentration in mega-cap tech.

Meanwhile, CSWC has exposure to short-term interest rate risk, private credit sector concerns, and a dividend that could potentially be cut due to it not having significant dividend coverage.

However, I think the risk-reward profile for both of these as parts of a well-diversified portfolio that is supported by lower-yielding dividend-growing positions makes them worth owning for retirees looking for attractive monthly income. As a result, these are the sorts of opportunities that we look for when building our 7-8% yielding, total return outperforming, below-market beta retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Canadian Natural: Huge Gains, Dividend Increase, Earnings Beat
data.description:
Article Thesis

On Thursday, Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) announced its Q4 earnings results. The company beat estimates easily, showed strong business growth compared to one year earlier, and announced another dividend increase on top of that. In recent weeks, shares have risen nicely, and they are not the bargain they were a while ago -- but I remain bullish on Canadian Natural Resources.

Past Coverage

I have written about Canadian Natural Resources here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, when I published this article. I recommended buying CNQ back then, arguing that Venezuela-related fears were way overblown. So far, this thesis has worked out well, as Canadian Natural Resources has risen by a very nice 30% in the ~2 months since that article was published. With the company releasing its most recent earnings results on Thursday, it's time for me to update my views on the company today.

What Happened?

On Wednesday morning, Canadian Natural Resources announced its Q4 earnings results. The key numbers looked like this:

We see that CNQ outperformed the analyst consensus widely when it comes to its earnings per share, with the C$0.82 result being a hefty 17% higher than expected. That being said, net profits and earnings per share are not the best metrics to evaluate a company like CNQ, I believe -- high non-cash depreciation charges for its oil sands mining assets make net profits look rather low compared to cash flows, which is why I think that looking at cash flow/funds flow is ideal.

Canadian Natural Resources: Tailwinds From Higher Oil Prices

Escalation in the Middle East has made oil prices jump significantly over the last couple of days, with investors fearing supply disruptions for oil exporters that ship oil through the Strait of Hormuz. Higher oil prices are, all else equal, positive for oil producers, including Canadian Natural Resources. With its assets being located in North America primarily, Canadian Natural Resources is also pretty safe from any potential Iranian attacks. Higher oil prices, which should result in higher revenues and higher earnings, are good for CNQ, and with Canadian oil suppliers being seen as comparatively safe, CNQ benefits doubly -- it's thus no wonder that its shares gained nicely over the last couple of trading sessions, adding to already very solid year-to-date gains.

We do not yet know how long the current conflict involving Iran will continue, and how large the Strait of Hormuz supply disruptions will get. It's thus not possible to forecast how high oil will eventually run, and how long it will stay there. But it seems quite likely that the current conflict will give at least a temporary boost to the profits of CNQ and other oil companies, and oil investors may price in a higher geopolitical risk premium in the medium term even after the current conflict has ended.

Canadian Natural Resources' Q4: Strong Performance

Getting back to Canadian Natural Resources' Q4 report, we see that production is up very nicely versus one year earlier, with CNQ's daily output climbing to 1.66 million barrels per day, which was a new record. This was 13% more than one quarter earlier, with the year-over-year increase being comparable. Production growth was driven by both organic investments, e.g., in debottlenecking at existing assets, and M&A -- Canadian Natural Resources buys assets regularly, with 2025 purchases including the Palliser Block deal and the Montney deal. One can argue whether going for production growth was a great strategy a couple of months ago, when Brent (CO1:COM) traded in the low $60s, but with Brent trading in the mid-$80s following the recent price jump, Canadian Natural Resources' production growth strategy looks quite compelling.

During the fourth quarter, for which we just got the report, oil prices were still rather low -- and still, Canadian Natural Resources generated compelling cash flows. That is possible thanks to low break-even prices and long-lived assets with low decline rates, so not too much capital spending in "replacement assets" is needed to keep production from falling.

Canadian Natural Resources' adjusted funds flows, where one-time items that distort the picture are backed out, totaled C$3.75 billion during the fourth quarter, which makes for an annual pace of C$15 billion -- before the recent oil price increase. Net capital expenditures totaled C$1.24 billion during the fourth quarter, which means that free funds flow came in at just above C$2.5 billion, or C$10 billion on an annualized basis. Canadian Natural Resources is currently valued at C$125 billion, so its free funds flow multiple, based on the Q4 free funds generation pace, stands at 12.5x. That's not as cheap as CNQ was last year, but it isn't a high valuation, either -- and the Q4 cash flow numbers do not yet account for the significant oil price increase we have seen so far in 2026. If those oil prices hold, it is extremely likely that Canadian Natural Resources will generate much higher cash flows this year compared to what the Q4 run rate would suggest. This, in turn, would mean that the "true" valuation is lower than what the Q4 numbers suggest, of course.

CNQ's New Capital Allocation Framework

Like many other energy companies, Canadian Natural Resources operates with a capital allocation framework that dictates that certain percentages of its free cash flows will be directed towards shareholders and debt reduction, depending on how indebted the company is at a specific time. This makes sense, I think, as shareholders know what they will get and as management has "guidelines" so there's no need for guessing what the ideal capital allocation might be at a specific time.

Since Canadian Natural Resources has grown considerably over the last couple of years, due to M&A and organic investments, the company felt that it was time to adjust the capital allocation framework. Instead of aiming for a net debt level of C$12 billion, CNQ now aims for a net debt level of C$13 billion, and it will return 60% of its free cash flow to its owners as soon as its net debt sinks below C$16 billion, versus that threshold being C$15 billion under the old capital allocation framework. In other words, Canadian Natural Resources now feels better about somewhat higher debt levels -- which makes sense, as EBITDA, cash flows, production, etc. have grown -- and it will return more money to its owners sooner. As a shareholder, I like that, of course, although it's important to note that share repurchases are now less efficient than they were last year, as CNQ trades at a higher share price and valuation.

Canadian Natural Resources also announced another dividend increase, lifting the payout by 6% to C$0.625 per share per quarter, or C$2.50 per year. Between a ~4% yield and a 6% dividend growth rate, Canadian Natural Resources remains a compelling dividend growth pick, I think, although buying it at a higher going-in yield last year was even better compared to buying it today.

Takeaway

Canadian Natural Resources had another strong quarter, showing substantial production growth, strong cash generation, and debt reduction in Q4. That was possible despite rather low oil prices -- with oil prices rising substantially so far this year, Q1 and beyond could be even better. A dividend increase and a revised capital allocation framework -- more cash is going to shareholders sooner -- round out the positives. If oil prices remain high or rise further due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, Canadian Natural Resources could climb higher, like other energy companies, but it is, of course, also possible that this conflict ends soon and that oil prices pull back again. In the long run, investors will do well with CNQ, I believe, and I remain long -- but buying a couple of months ago made for an even better deal than buying today, following nice year-to-date gains.

data.title: Boosting After-Tax Returns: An 'All Of The Above' Effort
data.description:
By Paul Robertson | Gavin Romm, CFA | Mark Gleason, CFA

Better performance requires more than just focusing on pretax returns or minimizing tax expenses.

Improving after-tax returns is rarely as simple as boosting pretax returns or reducing tax expenses. It’s actually quite a bit more involved than that. As we see it, maximizing after-tax performance requires an “all of the above” approach, applying a range of techniques in a holistic way.

For investors, improving the path of returns via better up-down capture may drive better outcomes. Sources can be incorporating better building blocks, assembling them more efficiently, and adding alpha to enhance market returns. For investors with assets in taxable accounts, a sometimes-forgotten alpha source can be boosting after-tax returns—and that means reducing the tax bite.

On the surface of it, the math of after-tax return is simple. Take your returns before taxes, subtract the tax expenses you owe, and there you have it. But that seeming simplicity often leads people to narrow their focus to two variables: 1) boosting returns before taxes and 2) reducing tax expenses. But as always seems the case with taxes, it’s not that simple.

The After-Tax Return Equation Is More Complicated

There seems to be a never-ending stream of research into the ways investors can boost pretax returns, often by analyzing new data sets using new techniques, seeking to find patterns to unlock higher pretax returns. But those improvements aren’t a lock to flow through to the after-tax bottom line.

In fact, they might lead to steeper tax expenses—and the tax on short-term capital gains is a major culprit. Strategies designed to exploit high-frequency data sets are very likely to buy and sell securities quickly. That rapid turnover can create short-term capital gains taxed at a higher rate. The challenge with boosting pretax returns is to find a strategy that boosts returns enough to offset extra taxes.

The other variable in keeping more after taxes is to reduce tax expenses by taking advantage of opportunities in the tax code, and there’s an impressive inventory of tools to work with, spanning investment types, trading strategy and even investment vehicles.

Invest in municipal bonds. Muni bonds have a built-in tax advantage versus taxable bonds, such as corporate debt. Interest income from muni bonds isn’t subject to federal income taxes, and if the bonds are issued in an investor's home state, state taxes may not apply either. Taxable bonds, on the other hand, are subject to federal income taxes and most of them to state income taxes, too (Display).

Reduce portfolio turnover by holding investments longer. Less buying and selling reduce taxable transactions, and investments held for more than a year face a lower capital gains tax rate when sold. The top federal long-term capital gains tax rate is 20% (23.8% including the Medicare surtax); for short-term gains, it’s a whopping 37% (40.8% including the Medicare surtax). Likewise, qualified stock dividends (that meet a holding-period test) are taxed at the long-term capital gains rate; non-qualified dividends face a much higher tax rate—the same as short-term capital gains.

Tax Rates Make a Big Difference in Both Gains and Income

Representative Tax Rates for Capital Gains and Income (Percent)

Offset realized capital gains with realized capital losses. At times, investors need to sell securities, which could produce a realized capital gain. This prompts many investors to hurriedly scan their portfolios at the end of the year, looking for other holdings that are in the red and can be sold at a loss to reduce their yearly net capital gains tax expenses.

There’s the option of systematically slowing portfolio turnover and taking realized tax losses in a tax-management overlay strategy for strategies in separately managed accounts (SMAs). But implemented ineffectively, this could inhibit active management, ultimately leaving less pretax return to preserve.

Opt for tax-friendly vehicles. Tax-deferred accounts like individual retirement accounts or 401(k) accounts can protect assets from taxes, but their contribution limits require investors to reduce taxes on assets elsewhere. With partnerships or SMAs, net capital losses can be made available for investors to use immediately, offsetting realized gains from other securities. Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) offer tax advantages through their creation and redemption processes, which may defer capital gains for investors. Strategies likely to produce realized capital gains regularly might be better suited for an ETF; for those likely to generate no capital gains or to produce capital losses (such as direct-index tax-loss harvesting equity strategies and tax-managed munis), SMAs or partnerships could be the better fit.

Asking Questions to Size Up the Potential Tax Bite

If it seems like there are a lot of moving parts in tax management, there are. Improving after-tax returns is rarely as simple as increasing returns before taxes or paring back tax expenses. In our view, a holistic, “all of the above” approach makes sense. Investors with taxable assets should ask pointed questions when considering their options:

  • How does the investment strategy generate its return?
  • Does that approach result in high turnover (and possibly a higher tax profile)?
  • Will the taxable capital gains likely be mainly short term or long term?
  • Can the strategy accommodate a tax-management overlay?
  • Will that tax overlay have a significant impact on the strategy’s pretax returns?
  • Is the strategy available in a tax-efficient vehicle?

As we see it, taking home strong after-tax performance takes more than just focusing on pretax returns or minimizing tax expenses. A robust set of tax-management strategies, tailored to each investor's specific situation, may enable investors with taxable assets to keep more of what they earn.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: As Losses Endure, Babcock & Wilcox Has A Dilution And Cash Flow Problem (Downgrade)
data.description:
What a difference 18 months can make with a stock. Back in September of 2024, I initiated coverage on Babcock & Wilcox (BW) on the occasion of the company’s first quarter of earnings. EPS was only 1¢, and it wasn’t clear it was going to continue, so with uncertainty and cash flow weakness, I rated the formerly sub-$2 company a hold, advising investors to keep a close eye on the cash flow before making decisions. At present day, the stock is dramatically higher priced.

The stock surged late in 2025 and even further this week on news that they had come in ahead of estimates for Q4, and had received the go-ahead on a major new project, one that dwarfs the sort of backlog Babcock & Wilcox normally carries. The market cap is growing, but what about the company?

That’s a more complicated issue, and as we examine what’s happened from then to now, we need to revisit the struggling balance sheet, the cash flow issues, and some new potential problems on the horizon as the company tries to grow into a much bigger player with an AI data center electricity project.

Paying Down Debt, the Dilution Problem

The first new problem is dilution, and we’re going to start to look at that as we reexamine the company’s balance sheet. Back in 2024, Babcock & Wilcox was carrying a rather large amount of debt for a company their size and, conspicuously, also a deficit of equity, which is never a good sign. Today, things aren’t much better, and when you see how they managed to get “not much better,” you’ll see why dilution is a red flag.

Then

Now

Cash & Equivalents

$95 million

$89.5 million

Total Current Assets

$574.5 million

$470.7 million

Total Current Liabilities

$334 million

$386.7 million

Senior Notes

$339 million

$235 million

Total Shareholder Equity

($200 million)

($132 million)

Shares Outstanding

92.2 million

121.8 million

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

What happened here is pretty stark. The current ratio got somewhat worse, and we’ll see why that might matter in a bit, and there’s still a deficit of equity. Debt was down a bit, but it’s because some of the senior notes had to be paid off as they were coming due in Q4 of 2025. How did they pay it? They issued almost 30 million new shares of common stock, raising roughly $130 million.

You’ll notice also that the equity deficit shrank, but less than what it would’ve purely from a wildly dilutive issue of new shares. This is because the narrow profitability that Babcock & Wilcox enjoyed in Q3 of 2024 didn’t exactly last.

Return to Loss and the Cash Flow Problem

Babcock & Wilcox hasn’t been profitable on a GAAP basis, and while there were hopes that they’d reach non-GAAP profitability in 2025, as I mentioned in the last analysis, that didn’t happen. Rather, non-GAAP losses grew year-over-year.

2024

2025

Revenue

$581 million

$588 million

Operating Income

($6.4 million)

$20.8 million

Interest Expenses

($46.1 million)

($37.5 million)

Net Income

($74.8 million)

($51.0 million)

GAAP EPS

(82¢)

(48¢)

Non-GAAP EPS

(21¢)

(24¢)

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

From a GAAP perspective, things got slightly less bad here. Revenue didn’t meaningfully grow, but Babcock & Wilcox did manage to come up with something of an operating income, even if that was still quickly wiped out by the interest expenses they had to pay, and other non-operating expenses. In the end, the GAAP loss was smaller, but the non-GAAP loss was slightly bigger, which isn’t optimal.

Estimates for 2026 are hopeful that non-GAAP can get to slight profitability, with estimates of revenue of $723 million and EPS of 10¢, though I’d point out that back in September of 2024, the estimates at the time were that 2025 would enjoy revenue of $989 million and non-GAAP earnings of 13¢. As you can see, though they beat the estimates that were eventually offered after multiple downward revisions, Babcock & Wilcox was supposed to be non-GAAP profitable long ago, and they still aren’t there yet.

The operating income is an encouraging sign, taken on its own, but here again, the cash flow statement reveals it’s not as good as it seems.

2024

2025

Operating Cash Flow

($118.7 million)

($68.9 million)

Investing Cash Flow

$109.9 million

$197 million

Financing Cash Flow

$69.7 million

($58.7 million)

(Source: Press release from Q4 earnings by BW)

Once again, operations improved, but there’s still a lot of work left to do, as Babcock & Wilcox still didn’t manage to muster operating cash flow profitability. Two other problems stand out to me, though.

The first is that the only meaningful positive cash flow Babcock & Wilcox made in 2025 was from the sale of assets and parts of the business that they considered “unprofitable.” This is obviously unsustainable, because Babcock & Wilcox has more liabilities than assets, hence the negative equity on the balance sheet. Moreover, since the company in general isn’t operating profitably, arguably anything could fairly be called an “unprofitable” business segment.

The second is that financing cash flow ended up negative in 2025. Again, remember that section included a positive $130 million from issuing new shares of stock, and all that dilution was still dragged down to negative cash flow. That included paying down the debt somewhat, but the absence of operating profitability, I think, portends further needs to raise capital, and the reasons for that will become apparent in the next section.

The Base Electron Project: AI Data Center Power

The recent surge in stock price for Babcock & Wilcox comes with the announcement, concurrent with their Q4 earnings statement, that they’ve received a notice to proceed on a major project they’re doing with Base Electron. That project is valued at $2.4 billion, and involves four natural gas-fired boilers and turbines to provide electricity to major AI data center campuses belonging to Applied Digital (APLD).

It’s a massive project from Babcock & Wilcox’s perspective, as the company hasn’t normally been involved in projects numbered in the billions of dollars. Given how excited the market is about AI data centers these days, it should also come as no surprise that the market reacted very positively to the announcement.

That said, it remains an open question how much this will actually add to the bottom line. We don’t have a track record of Babcock & Wilcox doing projects anywhere near this size where we can reasonably guess the margin they might be able to realize on them. Similarly, since the margin on the projects they have been doing doesn’t boil down to a profit, it’s fair to ask whether this will make them serious money at all.

On top of that, the relative financial weakness of Babcock & Wilcox could be an issue here as well. This company is not structured to handle capital-intensive projects this large, because they never have before. If they need to raise more capital in the course of funding a project of this scope, that’s likely to be substantially further dilutive in the near term. The hope here is that this huge project will lead to other huge projects and that the huge projects will prove more profitable than the small projects historically have been. In the end, either is possible, but neither is guaranteed.

A Possible Route to Profitability but Backlog Problems

While I would say the underlying “risk” of my relatively bearish thesis is that Babcock & Wilcox could absolutely knock this project out of the park, secure a strong margin on it, and attract further substantial projects, right now the backlog shows another potential problem.

Backlog reported at year’s end

2023

$368.2 million

2024

$540.1 million

2025

$2.8 billion

(Source: 10-K from 2025 and press release from Q4 earnings)

The backlog growth from 2023 to 2024 was healthy growth in orders, and while Babcock & Wilcox didn’t end up turning a profit in the following fiscal year, it showed at the very least that there was healthy demand for the services they offer. The numbers at the end of 2025 suggest the opposite.

While the 2025 numbers are much larger, it’s entirely because the backlog includes the aforementioned $2.4 billion project. But for that project, the backlog actually got substantially weaker in 2025, and would only be in the realm of $400 million otherwise. This is a major concentration risk.

Even if the big project goes rather well from a margin perspective, it is at the moment the overwhelming majority of the backlog, so the company’s ability to gain future projects of that size is going to be an important indicator of how transformative the project will actually be.

Too Many Questions

When asking about how to handle my prior hold rating, I ask myself whether Babcock & Wilcox is currently worth the numbers it is trading at. Between the lack of equity, the lack of earnings, enormous dilution, and uncertainty about how well they will handle such an uncharacteristically large project, I feel the answer is no.

That’s why, for now, I’m downgrading Babcock & Wilcox to a strong sell, because for the company to justify its current level, a lot of things need to go not just right, but transformationally perfect. For a company not used to tackling projects on this scale, some hurdles seem virtually inevitable, and aren’t being priced in here.

AI data centers may be an exciting industry that gets the market talking, but the visibility on Babcock & Wilcox going through a project related to that to become a massively larger and more successful company than they are right now just isn’t there. The company might successfully navigate this opportunity into a successful venture, but the high price we’re already asked to pay suggests the market is getting far ahead of itself.

Investors should keep an eye on not just how the project is going, but how the backlog is faring in future quarters. Right now the company has a relative paucity of other things to work on after this big project, and that’s not the way this company will grow.
